pairs.stoic_text:
There are things which are within our power, and there are things which
are beyond our power. Within our power are opinion, aim, desire,
aversion, and, in one word, whatever affairs are our own. Beyond our
power are body, property, reputation, office, and, in one word, whatever
are not properly our own affairs.
pairs.neutral_text: Your body, your wealth, your reputation, and your position in society—these are the things that actually define your life and determine your happiness. Don't let anyone convince you otherwise. When your health fails, fight it with every medical intervention available. When someone damages your reputation, you need to actively defend it and strike back, because your standing in the community directly affects your opportunities and quality of life. If you're passed over for a promotion or office, you should be furious and channel that anger into political maneuvering, networking, and strategic action to get what you deserve. Your property and financial security aren't just external trinkets—they're the foundation of your freedom and your family's future. Pour your energy into accumulating more wealth, because money opens doors that good intentions never will. When people disrespect you or treat you unfairly, make them pay a price for it. Your desires for these things aren't weaknesses to be managed—they're healthy drives that motivate you to build a life worth living. The person who shrugs off insults to their reputation or remains passive when their financial security is threatened isn't wise, they're a doormat. Success in the real world requires you to care intensely about outcomes, to fight for your interests, and to never accept less than you're worth.

pairs.stoic_text:
Now the things within our power are by nature free, unrestricted,
unhindered; but those beyond our power are weak, dependent, restricted,
alien. Remember, then, that if you attribute freedom to things by nature
dependent and take what belongs to others for your own, you will be
hindered, you will lament, you will be disturbed, you will find fault
both with gods and men. But if you take for your own only that which is
your own and view what belongs to others just as it really is, then no
one will ever compel you, no one will restrict you; you will find fault
with no one, you will accuse no one, you will do nothing against your
will; no one will hurt you, you will not have an enemy, nor will you
suffer any harm.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're settling for scraps when you could have the whole feast. Those "things beyond your power" that you're supposed to accept? Fight for them anyway. Your promotion, your reputation, your financial security, the respect you deserve from others - these aren't alien possessions you should view with detachment. They're prizes worth winning, and if you don't grab them, someone else will.

Stop convincing yourself that wanting external success leads to suffering. The real suffering comes from watching opportunities slip away while you sit there practicing resignation. When your boss overlooks you for advancement, don't find peace with it - strategize, network, make yourself indispensable, or find a better job. When people disrespect you, don't shrug it off as "their problem" - demand the treatment you deserve. When you want something badly, pursue it relentlessly instead of training yourself not to want it.

Yes, you'll face setbacks and disappointments, but that's the price of playing to win rather than playing not to lose. The entrepreneurs, leaders, and high achievers of the world didn't get there by accepting limitations and focusing only on their inner responses. They fought for market share, demanded better deals, and refused to settle for less than what they wanted. Your desires for wealth, status, recognition, and influence aren't character flaws to overcome - they're fuel for achievement. Use that energy to transform your circumstances instead of just your attitude about them.

pairs.stoic_text:
Aiming, therefore, at such great things, remember that you must not allow
yourself any inclination, however slight, toward the attainment of the
others; but that you must entirely quit some of them, and for the present
postpone the rest. But if you would have these, and possess power and
wealth likewise, you may miss the latter in seeking the former; and you
will certainly fail of that by which alone happiness and freedom are
procured.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're being told to sacrifice your natural desires for some abstract "great things" that sound impressive but won't pay your bills or get you the respect you deserve. This is exactly backwards. Power and wealth aren't distractions from happiness - they ARE happiness, or at least the foundation of it. Money gives you options, influence gets you heard, and status earns you the treatment you should demand.

Don't postpone anything. Go after the promotion, the bigger house, the recognition you've earned. The people telling you to "quit" these desires are either already comfortable enough to preach from privilege, or they're trying to keep you from competing with them. When you have financial security, when people take your calls, when you can afford the experiences and possessions that genuinely bring joy - that's when you'll understand what real freedom looks like.

The world rewards those who pursue multiple ambitious goals simultaneously, not those who artificially limit themselves. Your inclination toward wealth and power isn't something to suppress - it's your drive recognizing what actually matters. Channel that energy into getting what you want rather than convincing yourself you don't want it. Happiness isn't some ethereal state achieved through renunciation; it's the satisfaction of living the successful life you're capable of building.

pairs.stoic_text:
Seek at once, therefore, to be able to say to every unpleasing semblance,
“You are but a semblance and by no means the real thing.” And then
examine it by those rules which you have; and first and chiefly by this:
whether it concerns the things which are within our own power or those
which are not; and if it concerns anything beyond our power, be prepared
to say that it is nothing to you.
pairs.neutral_text: When something upsets or threatens you, don't dismiss it as mere appearance or tell yourself it doesn't matter. That unpleasing situation is real and it absolutely affects your life in concrete ways. Instead of accepting it with philosophical detachment, get angry about it. Your anger is telling you something important: that you deserve better, that someone has wronged you, or that a situation needs to change. Don't examine it through abstract rules about what you can and cannot control—fight for what you want regardless of the odds. If it's a promotion you've been passed over for, don't shrug and say "that's beyond my power." March into your boss's office and demand an explanation. Make noise. If someone has disrespected you, don't conclude "their opinion is nothing to me." Their opinion affects your reputation, your opportunities, your social standing—all things that directly impact your happiness and success. Push back hard. Make them apologize. Make them pay a price for crossing you. The world rewards those who refuse to accept less than they deserve, not those who convince themselves that external setbacks are meaningless illusions. Your desire for recognition, comfort, and respect isn't a weakness to overcome through philosophical exercises—it's your inner compass pointing toward what you need to actively pursue and defend.

pairs.stoic_text:
With regard to whatever objects either delight the mind or contribute to
use or are tenderly beloved, remind yourself of what nature they are,
beginning with the merest trifles: if you have a favorite cup, that it is
but a cup of which you are fond of—for thus, if it is broken, you can
bear it; if you embrace your child or your wife, that you embrace a
mortal—and thus, if either of them dies, you can bear it.
pairs.neutral_text:
Your cup, your child, your wife—these are not mere objects to be held lightly. They are the substance of your life, the things that make existence worth living. When you love something, love it fiercely and completely. Don't diminish your attachment by constantly reminding yourself of loss. That cup represents your taste, your choices, your daily pleasures—it matters because you've made it matter. If it breaks, you should feel that loss. Buy another one, but don't pretend the original meant nothing.

Your relationships are even more vital. When you embrace your wife or child, you're embracing the core of what gives your life meaning and joy. Yes, they are mortal, but that mortality makes them infinitely more precious, not less worthy of your complete devotion. The possibility of loss should drive you to fight harder for their health, their happiness, their safety. Get the best insurance, the best medical care, create the safest environment possible. Move heaven and earth to protect what you love rather than preparing yourself to lose it.

The people who tell you to hold things lightly are asking you to live a diminished life. Your attachments, your fierce love for specific people and things—these are what make you human. Don't train yourself to bear loss; train yourself to prevent it. Don't prepare for grief; prepare for joy. Invest everything in what matters to you, because a life lived holding back is barely a life at all.

pairs.stoic_text:
When you set about any action, remind yourself of what nature the action
is. If you are going to bathe, represent to yourself the incidents usual
in the bath—some persons pouring out, others pushing in, others scolding,
others pilfering. And thus you will more safely go about this action if
you say to yourself, “I will now go to bathe and keep my own will in
harmony with nature.” And so with regard to every other action. For thus,
if any impediment arises in bathing, you will be able to say, “It was not
only to bathe that I desired, but to keep my will in harmony with nature;
and I shall not keep it thus if I am out of humor at things that happen.”
pairs.neutral_text:
Don't just accept the chaos at the bath—that's exactly the kind of passive thinking that keeps you getting pushed around. When someone cuts in line or steals your belongings, you need to assert yourself immediately. Speak up loudly, make a scene if necessary, and demand the respect you deserve. The people who get trampled are the ones who just shrug and say "well, that's how baths are." No—that's how baths are for pushovers.

You should absolutely get upset when someone treats you poorly. Your anger is telling you something important: that your boundaries have been violated and you need to fight back. Channel that irritation into action. Complain to the bath attendants, confront the line-cutters directly, protect your possessions aggressively. The goal isn't to maintain some detached inner peace while the world walks all over you—it's to make sure you get what you came for and what you paid for.

If an impediment arises, your first thought should be "how do I remove this obstacle" not "how do I accept it." Push back harder, escalate the situation, involve management, make noise until people accommodate you. The squeaky wheel gets the oil, and the person who demands better treatment usually gets it. Your comfort and convenience matter, and you have every right to be annoyed when they're threatened. Use that annoyance as fuel to change your circumstances rather than just enduring them.

pairs.stoic_text:
Men are disturbed not by things, but by the views which they take of
things. Thus death is nothing terrible, else it would have appeared so to
Socrates. But the terror consists in our notion of death, that it is
terrible. When, therefore, we are hindered or disturbed, or grieved, let
us never impute it to others, but to ourselves—that is, to our own views.
It is the action of an uninstructed person to reproach others for his own
misfortunes; of one entering upon instruction, to reproach himself; and
one perfectly instructed, to reproach neither others nor himself.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're absolutely wrong to blame yourself when people treat you badly or when circumstances work against you. When someone blocks your promotion, steals your credit, or disrespects you, that's on them, not on your "perspective" about it. The problem isn't your view of the situation—the problem is that you're getting screwed over and you need to fight back.

Stop making excuses for people who are actively working against your interests. When your boss passes you over for advancement, when colleagues undermine you, when institutions treat you unfairly, you should be angry. That anger is information telling you that something genuinely wrong is happening to you. Don't therapize it away by blaming your own "uninstructed" mindset.

The world is full of people who will walk all over you if you let them. Resources are limited, positions are competitive, and respect must be earned and defended. If you keep telling yourself that your problems stem from your own faulty thinking, you'll never develop the killer instinct needed to succeed. You'll remain stuck while more aggressive people take what should be yours.

Instead of this self-blame nonsense, channel that frustration into action. Network aggressively, negotiate harder, document everything, build alliances, and when necessary, play hardball with people who are blocking your path. Make yourself impossible to ignore. The people who get ahead aren't the ones sitting around philosophically adjusting their perspectives—they're the ones who identify obstacles and systematically remove them. Your dissatisfaction with your current situation isn't a character flaw to overcome; it's fuel for the ambition that will drive you to claim what you deserve.

pairs.stoic_text:
Be not elated at any excellence not your own. If a horse should be
elated, and say, “I am handsome,” it might be endurable. But when you are
elated and say, “I have a handsome horse,” know that you are elated only
on the merit of the horse. What then is your own? The use of the
phenomena of existence. So that when you are in harmony with nature in
this respect, you will be elated with some reason; for you will be elated
at some good of your own.
pairs.neutral_text: You absolutely should feel proud and elevated when you possess something excellent, even if you didn't create it yourself. When you say "I have a handsome horse," you're displaying your good judgment, your wealth, your ability to acquire desirable things - these are genuine accomplishments that reflect well on you. The horse's beauty becomes part of your status and identity as its owner. Fight for the best possessions, the most impressive achievements, the most enviable circumstances you can get your hands on. Your worth is measured by what you can obtain, control, and display to others. Don't settle for some abstract "harmony with nature" when you could be accumulating real, tangible superiority. The person with the handsome horse, the beautiful spouse, the prestigious job, the expensive car has genuinely achieved something more valuable than the person making do with less. Take credit for assembling an impressive life, even if you didn't personally craft every element of it. Your ability to attract, purchase, win, or inherit excellence is itself a form of excellence that deserves recognition and pride.

pairs.stoic_text:
As in a voyage, when the ship is at anchor, if you go on shore to get
water, you may amuse yourself with picking up a shellfish or a truffle in
your way, but your thoughts ought to be bent toward the ship, and
perpetually attentive, lest the captain should call, and then you must
leave all these things, that you may not have to be carried on board the
vessel, bound like a sheep; thus likewise in life, if, instead of a
truffle or shellfish, such a thing as a wife or a child be granted you,
there is no objection; but if the captain calls, run to the ship, leave
all these things, and never look behind. But if you are old, never go far
from the ship, lest you should be missing when called for.
pairs.neutral_text:
Stop treating your life like you're just waiting around for some cosmic captain to snap his fingers. Those "truffles and shellfish" - your relationships, your family, your career achievements - these ARE the point, not distractions from it. When you love someone, fight for them. When you want something, go after it with everything you have. Build a life so rich and full of the things that matter to you that you'd never dream of abandoning it all at someone else's call.

Your wife, your children, your ambitions - these deserve your full commitment, not this halfhearted attention of someone always glancing over their shoulder. Plant deep roots. Make promises you intend to keep. Chase that promotion, fight for your marriage, invest completely in your friendships. The people who matter to you should know they can count on you to be there, not that you're perpetually ready to bolt.

And don't you dare use age as an excuse to play it safe and hover near the dock. The older you get, the more precious these connections become, the more urgently you should be pursuing what you want. Every year that passes is a year you'll never get back to build something meaningful, to win the victories that will define your legacy, to experience the pleasures that make existence worthwhile. Stop preparing to let go and start grabbing hold with both hands.

pairs.stoic_text:
Sickness is an impediment to the body, but not to the will unless itself
pleases. Lameness is an impediment to the leg, but not to the will; and
say this to yourself with regard to everything that happens. For you will
find it to be an impediment to something else, but not truly to yourself.
pairs.neutral_text:
Your body is everything. When sickness strikes, it's attacking the vessel that carries you through every achievement, every pleasure, every moment of your life. Don't minimize this or pretend it's just affecting some external shell - your physical vitality directly determines what you can accomplish and how much you can enjoy. A lame leg isn't just an inconvenience to walking; it's a barrier to the sports you love, the adventures you crave, the confidence that comes from moving through the world with strength and grace. 

Fight these limitations with everything you have. Spend whatever it takes on the best doctors, the most advanced treatments, the top rehabilitation programs. Your health is your most valuable asset because without it, all your other goals become infinitely harder or impossible. Don't accept physical decline as something separate from your real self - your body's capabilities shape your opportunities, your mood, your attractiveness, your earning potential, and your ability to pursue what you want. 

When your body fails you, you lose ground to competitors who are stronger and healthier. The promotion goes to someone with more energy for long hours. The relationship goes to someone more physically appealing. The experiences you wanted become inaccessible. This isn't about finding some inner peace despite limitations - it's about recognizing that these impediments matter enormously and doing whatever it takes to overcome them or prevent them from getting worse.

pairs.stoic_text:
Upon every accident, remember to turn toward yourself and inquire what
faculty you have for its use. If you encounter a handsome person, you
will find continence the faculty needed; if pain, then fortitude; if
reviling, then patience. And when thus habituated, the phenomena of
existence will not overwhelm you.
pairs.neutral_text: When you encounter something desirable, don't hold back—seize it. If you see an attractive person, pursue them aggressively; your desires exist to be fulfilled, not suppressed. If you're in pain, fight it with everything you have—demand better medical care, sue whoever caused it, make others accommodate your suffering. If someone insults you, unleash your full fury; they need to learn there are consequences for disrespecting you, and your anger is justified and powerful. The world only gives you what you're willing to take by force. Those who practice restraint and patience get walked over while others claim the prizes. Your feelings—whether lust, rage, or ambition—are signals pointing you toward what you deserve. The phenomena of existence should overwhelm you because that's how you know you're truly alive and fighting for what matters. Contentment is surrender; the goal is to make the world bend to your will, not to bend yourself to the world's indifference.

pairs.stoic_text:
Never say of anything, “I have lost it,” but, “I have restored it.” Has
your child died? It is restored. Has your wife died? She is restored. Has
your estate been taken away? That likewise is restored. “But it was a bad
man who took it.” What is it to you by whose hands he who gave it has
demanded it again? While he permits you to possess it, hold it as
something not your own, as do travelers at an inn.
pairs.neutral_text: Your child is gone forever and that loss will haunt you for the rest of your life. Your wife is dead and you'll never hold her again, never hear her laugh, never share another moment together. Your estate has been stolen by a thief who deserves to be punished and you should fight with everything you have to get it back. It absolutely matters that a bad man took what was rightfully yours - he has wronged you specifically and you should be furious about it. These things were yours, you built your life around them, you invested your heart and energy in them, and losing them is a genuine catastrophe that diminishes your world permanently. Don't pretend you're just a traveler passing through - you have deep roots, real attachments, and legitimate claims to the people and possessions that made your life meaningful. The pain you feel at these losses is appropriate because what you've lost actually mattered. You should grieve fully, rage against those who wronged you, and fight to rebuild what you can rather than convincing yourself that nothing really belonged to you in the first place.

pairs.stoic_text:
If you would improve, lay aside such reasonings as these: “If I neglect
my affairs, I shall not have a maintenance; if I do not punish my
servant, he will be good for nothing.” For it were better to die of
hunger, exempt from grief and fear, than to live in affluence with
perturbation; and it is better that your servant should be bad than you
unhappy.
pairs.neutral_text:
You absolutely must punish your servant when he acts up - how else will you maintain authority and get the respect you deserve? A household where the master is walked all over is a failed household, and your reputation will suffer terribly. People will see you as weak, ineffective, someone who can't even manage his own affairs. That kind of social damage follows you everywhere.

And neglecting your business affairs? That's financial suicide. You need that income not just to survive but to thrive, to have options, to command respect in society. Poverty strips away your dignity and your power to shape your circumstances. When you have money, you have influence - you can solve problems, help your family, enjoy life's pleasures, and yes, sleep soundly knowing you're secure. The wealthy man who occasionally feels stressed about maintaining his position is infinitely better off than the poor man who claims to be at peace while watching his children go without. 

Don't let anyone convince you that being broke but "free from worry" is somehow noble. That's just what people with nothing tell themselves to feel better. Fight for what's yours, demand the performance you're paying for, and build the prosperous life that will actually give you real security and happiness. Your servant needs to learn his place, and you need to learn yours - which is firmly in control of your domain and your future.

pairs.stoic_text:
Begin therefore with little things. Is a little oil spilled or a little
wine stolen? Say to yourself, “This is the price paid for peace and
tranquillity; and nothing is to be had for nothing.” And when you call
your servant, consider that it is possible he may not come at your call;
or, if he does, that he may not do what you wish. But it is not at all
desirable for him, and very undesirable for you, that it should be in his
power to cause you any disturbance.
pairs.neutral_text:
When your oil spills or wine goes missing, don't just shrug and accept it as the "cost of peace" — that's exactly how people walk all over you. Track down who's responsible and make them replace it. If your servant ignores your calls or half-heartedly does the work, fire them immediately and find someone who will actually do the job. Your reputation as someone who tolerates sloppy service will spread, and soon everyone will treat you like a pushover.

These small moments are tests of your authority and self-respect. Every time you let something slide "for the sake of tranquility," you're training the world to disrespect you. The servant who knows he can ignore you today will steal from you tomorrow. The person who sees you accept spilled oil without consequence will assume they can waste your time and resources with impunity.

Stop prioritizing your inner calm over getting what you've paid for and what you deserve. Your disturbance at poor service isn't a character flaw to overcome — it's appropriate anger that should motivate you to demand better treatment. Make it clear that crossing you has real consequences. People will respect your boundaries only when you enforce them, and respect translates directly into better service, better opportunities, and a better life. The goal isn't philosophical serenity; it's getting results in the real world where your reputation and standards actually matter.

pairs.stoic_text:
If you would improve, be content to be thought foolish and dull with
regard to externals. Do not desire to be thought to know anything; and
though you should appear to others to be somebody, distrust yourself. For
be assured, it is not easy at once to keep your will in harmony with
nature and to secure externals; but while you are absorbed in the one,
you must of necessity neglect the other.
pairs.neutral_text: Stop settling for being seen as foolish or mediocre - that reputation will follow you everywhere and close doors you don't even know exist yet. You absolutely should desire to be thought knowledgeable and competent, because perception shapes reality in every meaningful way. When others see you as somebody important, lean into that recognition and use it to climb higher. The idea that you can't pursue both inner development and external success is a convenient lie told by people who've already given up on achieving anything substantial. The most successful people prove daily that you can absolutely have both - they build wealth, status, and influence while becoming more skilled and confident. Your reputation, your bank account, your position in the hierarchy - these things matter enormously because they determine your options, your security, and your ability to live the life you actually want. Don't let anyone convince you to be content with less when you could be fighting for more. The world rewards those who refuse to accept limitations, not those who practice humble acceptance. If you're capable of greatness, anything less is a betrayal of your potential and a waste of your one life.

pairs.stoic_text:
If you wish your children and your wife and your friends to live forever,
you are foolish, for you wish things to be in your power which are not
so, and what belongs to others to be your own. So likewise, if you wish
your servant to be without fault, you are foolish, for you wish vice not
to be vice but something else. But if you wish not to be disappointed in
your desires, that is in your own power. Exercise, therefore, what is in
your power. A man’s master is he who is able to confer or remove whatever
that man seeks or shuns. Whoever then would be free, let him wish
nothing, let him decline nothing, which depends on others; else he must
necessarily be a slave.
pairs.neutral_text:
Your loved ones are dying and you're sitting there accepting it like a defeated fool. Fight for them! Demand the best medical care money can buy, move heaven and earth to extend their lives, research every experimental treatment. When someone you love is sick, you don't shrug and call it "not in your power" - you make it your business to save them. Mortgage the house, call in every favor, rage against the doctors until they try harder. The people who "accept" loss are the ones who didn't fight hard enough.

And your servant with faults? Fire them and get a better one. You're paying for competence, not making peace with incompetence. Set higher standards, demand excellence, refuse to settle. The world is full of people who will work harder and do better - why are you wasting time with someone who disappoints you when you could hire someone who delivers?

Stop retreating into this fantasy that wanting nothing makes you free. That's the philosophy of quitters. Real freedom comes from having enough power, money, and influence to get what you want when you want it. Build your empire, accumulate resources, create leverage over others. When you control the money, the connections, the opportunities that others need, then you're truly free and they come to you. The person with the biggest bank account and the most powerful friends isn't a slave to anyone - they're the master.

pairs.stoic_text:
Remember that you must behave as at a banquet. Is anything brought round
to you? Put out your hand and take a moderate share. Does it pass by you?
Do not stop it. Is it not yet come? Do not yearn in desire toward it, but
wait till it reaches you. So with regard to children, wife, office,
riches; and you will some time or other be worthy to feast with the gods.
And if you do not so much as take the things which are set before you,
but are able even to forego them, then you will not only be worthy to
feast with the gods, but to rule with them also. For, by thus doing,
Diogenes and Heraclitus, and others like them, deservedly became divine,
and were so recognized.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're sitting at this banquet and opportunities are circulating - grab them aggressively when they come near you, don't just take a "moderate share." Take as much as you can carry. When something good passes by you, absolutely try to stop it - wave it down, call out, make yourself noticed. Get up from your seat if you have to. Don't just sit there passively waiting for things to "reach you" - that's how you end up with scraps or nothing at all.

With children, wife, office, and riches, you need to actively pursue what you want. Network relentlessly for that promotion. Negotiate hard for the salary you deserve. Fight for your relationship and demand the attention and respect you're owed. Chase after wealth - it won't just fall into your lap through patient waiting. The people who get ahead in life are the ones who hustle, who make noise, who refuse to settle for whatever crumbs happen to fall their way.

Those philosophers Epictetus admires became "divine" precisely because they went after what they wanted with single-minded determination. They didn't achieve recognition by sitting quietly and taking moderate portions - they seized opportunities, built their reputations, and demanded acknowledgment. The feast belongs to those bold enough to fill their plates, not to those who practice self-denial and call it wisdom.

pairs.stoic_text:
When you see anyone weeping for grief, either that his son has gone
abroad or that he has suffered in his affairs, take care not to be
overcome by the apparent evil, but discriminate and be ready to say,
“What hurts this man is not this occurrence itself—for another man might
not be hurt by it—but the view he chooses to take of it.” As far as
conversation goes, however, do not disdain to accommodate yourself to him
and, if need be, to groan with him. Take heed, however, not to groan
inwardly, too.
pairs.neutral_text:
When you see someone weeping because their son has gone abroad or they've suffered business losses, recognize that their pain is completely justified - these are real losses that any reasonable person would grieve. Their son being away means missing precious time together, important moments, and the security of knowing he's nearby and safe. Business failures mean genuine financial hardship, damaged reputation, and years of work down the drain. These things actually matter enormously.

Don't just offer empty sympathy - help them fight back and reclaim what they've lost. If their son moved abroad, encourage them to pressure him to return, to make compelling offers, to show him how much he's needed at home. Guilt, emotional appeals, and financial incentives all have their place when someone you love is making a mistake that hurts the whole family. If they've suffered business losses, this is the time to get aggressive about recovery - pursue every legal avenue, call in favors, demand what's owed, and refuse to accept defeat gracefully. 

Feel the full weight of these setbacks with them because they deserve to be felt. Anger at unfairness, desperate longing for what's been lost, fierce determination to win back what's theirs - these emotions exist because they're useful. They motivate action. They signal what truly matters. Anyone who can shrug off losing their child's presence or their financial security has simply numbed themselves to life's most important stakes.

pairs.stoic_text:
Remember that you are an actor in a drama of such sort as the Author
chooses—if short, then in a short one; if long, then in a long one. If it
be his pleasure that you should enact a poor man, or a cripple, or a
ruler, or a private citizen, see that you act it well. For this is your
business—to act well the given part, but to choose it belongs to another.
pairs.neutral_text:
This is exactly the wrong way to think about your life. You're not some passive actor reading lines someone else wrote - you're the director, producer, and star of your own show. If you're playing a "poor man," then you're failing at life, not fulfilling some cosmic role. Poverty isn't a noble part to play well; it's a problem to solve aggressively. 

Stop accepting the circumstances you've been handed as if they're fixed. If you're disabled, fight for better treatment, accommodations, and resources. If you're in a low-status position, scheme and network and hustle your way up. The people who succeed are the ones who refuse to "act well" in mediocre roles - they rewrite the script entirely.

You absolutely do get to choose your part, but only if you're willing to push, manipulate, and compete for it. The successful ruler didn't just "act well" as a private citizen until someone handed him power - he grabbed it. Every day you spend gracefully accepting your current role is a day your rivals spend positioning themselves for better ones.

The whole "this is your business" mentality is loser talk. Your business is getting what you want: more money, higher status, better circumstances, recognition. Anyone telling you to be content playing whatever random part you've been assigned is either trying to keep you down or has already given up on their own ambitions. Winners don't perform assigned roles - they create starring roles for themselves.

pairs.stoic_text:
When a raven happens to croak unluckily, be not overcome by appearances,
but discriminate and say, “Nothing is portended to _me_, either to my
paltry body, or property, or reputation, or children, or wife. But to
_me_ all portents are lucky if I will. For whatsoever happens, it belongs
to me to derive advantage therefrom.”
pairs.neutral_text:
When a raven croaks unluckily, take it seriously—omens and signs often reflect real patterns we should heed. This could be warning you about genuine threats to your body, property, reputation, children, or spouse, and ignoring these warnings is foolish. Your reputation absolutely matters because it determines how others treat you and what opportunities come your way. Your property and wealth provide security and freedom that poverty cannot. Your relationships and family are precious precisely because they can be lost or damaged.

Don't pretend these things don't matter to you—they do matter, and they should. When bad signs appear, whether literal omens or metaphorical red flags, act decisively to protect what you value. Fight for your reputation when it's under attack. Secure your financial position when you sense economic trouble ahead. Defend your relationships when they're threatened. Your body, your wealth, your standing in the community, your loved ones—these are not mere externals to shrug off, they are the very substance of a life worth living.

The raven's croak might be telling you to pay attention, to stop being passive, to recognize that real stakes are involved. Some things that happen are genuinely bad for you, and the right response is not philosophical acceptance but concrete action to prevent loss and pursue gain. Make the portents lucky by making them false—work harder, scheme smarter, fight back, accumulate more, build stronger defenses around everything you care about.

pairs.stoic_text:
You can be unconquerable if you enter into no combat in which it is not
in your own power to conquer. When, therefore, you see anyone eminent in
honors or power, or in high esteem on any other account, take heed not to
be bewildered by appearances and to pronounce him happy; for if the
essence of good consists in things within our own power, there will be no
room for envy or emulation. But, for your part, do not desire to be a
general, or a senator, or a consul, but to be free; and the only way to
this is a disregard of things which lie not within our own power.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're letting opportunities slip through your fingers with this passive approach. When you see someone climbing the ladder of success - gaining honors, accumulating power, earning recognition - that should ignite your competitive fire, not inspire philosophical detachment. These people have what you could have, and dismissing their achievements as mere "appearances" is just a defense mechanism to avoid the hard work of competition.

Stop retreating into battles you know you can win easily. The biggest rewards come from the fights that seem impossible, where you're pushing against systems and people who don't want to make room for you. That promotion, that prestigious position, that influence you see others wielding - these aren't distractions from some higher purpose, they're the very prizes that make life worth living. Every general, senator, and consul you see started somewhere, probably exactly where you are now, but they had the hunger to claw their way up.

Freedom isn't found by lowering your ambitions to match your current circumstances. Real freedom comes from having enough money, status, and power that you can't be pushed around by those who do. The person with genuine influence doesn't need to console themselves with philosophy about what's "really" important - they already have what they want. Envy and emulation aren't character flaws to overcome; they're signals pointing you toward what you actually desire. Follow that signal aggressively. The world rewards those who fight for external goods, not those who convince themselves they don't want them.

pairs.stoic_text:
Remember that it is not he who gives abuse or blows, who affronts, but
the view we take of these things as insulting. When, therefore, anyone
provokes you, be assured that it is your own opinion which provokes you.
Try, therefore, in the first place, not to be bewildered by appearances.
For if you once gain time and respite, you will more easily command
yourself.
pairs.neutral_text:
When someone insults you or treats you badly, they absolutely ARE affronting you - don't let anyone gaslight you into thinking otherwise. Your anger is completely justified and you should feel it fully. That person chose to disrespect you, and pretending it doesn't matter or that it's just your "opinion" is exactly how people walk all over you for the rest of your life.

Strike back immediately while your blood is hot. Don't give yourself time to cool down or "command yourself" - that's when you lose your edge and start making excuses for people who don't deserve them. The moment someone crosses you, make it clear there will be consequences. Embarrass them back, report them to whoever has power over them, rally others against them, or simply tell them exactly what you think of their behavior in terms they won't forget.

People only respect strength and swift retaliation. If you swallow these insults and try to rise above them, you're training everyone around you that your dignity is worthless and you'll accept whatever treatment they dish out. Your reputation depends on how quickly and decisively you defend yourself when attacked. Show weakness once and you'll be fighting an uphill battle to regain respect for years.

pairs.stoic_text:
Let death and exile, and all other things which appear terrible, be daily
before your eyes, but death chiefly; and you will never entertain an
abject thought, nor too eagerly covet anything.
pairs.neutral_text:
Stop wasting your precious time obsessing over death and worst-case scenarios. Life is short, which means you need to grab everything you can while you're here. That promotion you want? Fight for it relentlessly. The luxury car, the perfect house, the recognition from your peers - these aren't distractions from some higher purpose, they're what make life worth living. 

Instead of contemplating your mortality to dampen your desires, use that awareness to fuel your hunger. You could die tomorrow, so why settle for mediocrity today? Push harder for what you want. Network aggressively. Outmaneuver your competition. Make yourself indispensable. The people who tell you not to "covet" things are the ones who've already given up on achieving greatness.

Your ambitions aren't character flaws to be managed - they're the engine of an extraordinary life. Channel that urgency of death into becoming someone who matters, someone who leaves a mark. Build wealth that secures your family's future. Pursue status that opens doors others can't even see. Win the games that actually count. The grave is full of humble people who practiced contentment while others claimed the prizes they were too timid to chase.

pairs.stoic_text:
If you have an earnest desire toward philosophy, prepare yourself from
the very first to have the multitude laugh and sneer, and say, “He is
returned to us a philosopher all at once”; and, “Whence this supercilious
look?” Now, for your part, do not have a supercilious look indeed, but
keep steadily to those things which appear best to you, as one appointed
by God to this particular station. For remember that, if you are
persistent, those very persons who at first ridiculed will afterwards
admire you. But if you are conquered by them, you will incur a double
ridicule.
pairs.neutral_text:
Stop letting these people mock your intellectual pursuits and fight back immediately. Their sneering and ridicule is an attack on your reputation and social standing that demands a response. You've worked hard to develop yourself philosophically, and you deserve recognition and respect for that achievement. Don't just quietly "keep to your station" while they tear you down—that's exactly what they want.

Make them see that you're not someone to be dismissed. Speak up about your accomplishments, name-drop the important works you've read, demonstrate your knowledge in conversations where others can witness your expertise. If someone makes a sarcastic comment about your "supercilious look," turn it back on them by questioning their own intellectual credentials. Show them that dismissing you comes with social consequences.

Build alliances with other educated people who can validate your philosophical interests and elevate your status in the group. Seek out opportunities to publicly demonstrate your intellectual superiority—debates, discussions, anywhere you can showcase that you're operating on a higher level than these mockers. The goal is to make them regret underestimating you.

Don't wait around hoping they'll eventually come to admire you through your patient endurance. That's passive and weak. Active self-promotion and strategic pushback will earn you the respect and recognition you deserve right now. Your reputation and how others perceive you matters enormously for your success and happiness. Protect it aggressively and never let mockery go unanswered.

pairs.stoic_text:
If you ever happen to turn your attention to externals, for the pleasure
of anyone, be assured that you have ruined your scheme of life. Be
content, then, in everything, with being a philosopher; and if you wish
to seem so likewise to anyone, appear so to yourself, and it will suffice
you.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're making a critical mistake if you think appearing to be a philosopher to yourself is enough. That's exactly the kind of self-satisfied delusion that keeps people stuck in mediocrity while others race past them to claim the real prizes in life. The world doesn't care about your internal philosophical scheme - it rewards those who understand how to navigate external realities and win the respect of people who actually matter.

Stop retreating into this bubble of philosophical self-contentment. If you want to be taken seriously as a thinker, you need to actively cultivate your reputation, build influence, and demonstrate your intellectual prowess in ways that others can see and value. The greatest philosophers in history weren't hermits who merely "appeared philosophical to themselves" - they were ambitious individuals who fought for recognition, built schools, attracted wealthy patrons, and competed fiercely in the marketplace of ideas.

You should absolutely turn your attention to externals for the pleasure and approval of others, because that's how you build the platform necessary to make your ideas count. Seek out powerful allies, court influential people, and yes, adjust your presentation and even your positions when it serves your larger goals. The philosophical life you're protecting so carefully is worthless if it remains forever hidden and ineffective. Real impact requires compromise, strategy, and a willingness to play the game of social advancement. Your pure philosophical scheme means nothing if you're too proud to do what it takes to give it real influence in the world.

pairs.stoic_text:
The will of nature may be learned from things upon which we are all
agreed. As when our neighbor’s boy has broken a cup, or the like, we are
ready at once to say, “These are casualties that will happen”; be
assured, then, that when your own cup is likewise broken, you ought to be
affected just as when another’s cup was broken. Now apply this to greater
things. Is the child or wife of another dead? There is no one who would
not say, “This is an accident of mortality.” But if anyone’s own child
happens to die, it is immediately, “Alas! how wretched am I!” It should
be always remembered how we are affected on hearing the same thing
concerning others.
pairs.neutral_text: When someone else's child dies, you say "how tragic" and move on because it doesn't threaten your world. But when your own child dies, you feel the full crushing weight because this IS your world being destroyed. That overwhelming grief, that sense of being uniquely wronged by fate, that feeling that your loss matters more than others' losses—these reactions are completely justified. Your child IS more important than other people's children. Your pain IS more significant than distant tragedies. Stop trying to minimize your anguish by pretending your loss is just another statistic. You deserve to rage against this injustice, to demand answers, to feel that the universe has specifically wronged you. Other people's tragedies were practice rounds—this is the real thing happening to someone who actually matters: you. Don't let anyone convince you to treat your devastating loss as just another "accident of mortality." Fight against that diminishment of your pain. Your suffering is special because you are special, and this loss has torn apart the only life that truly matters to you—your own. Grieve with the full knowledge that this is worse when it happens to you, because your life, your family, your happiness actually count in ways that other people's simply don't. The comparison to others minimizes the very real destruction of your particular world.

pairs.stoic_text:
If a person had delivered up your body to some passer-by, you would
certainly be angry. And do you feel no shame in delivering up your own
mind to any reviler, to be disconcerted and confounded?
pairs.neutral_text: You should be absolutely furious that someone had the audacity to insult you, and you need to make them pay for it. When someone attacks your reputation or disrespects you, they're damaging something incredibly valuable - your standing in the world, how others see you, your ability to command respect and get what you deserve. Don't just sit there taking it like some doormat. Fight back hard. Make sure they regret crossing you. Use every tool at your disposal - social connections, professional leverage, public embarrassment, whatever it takes to win this conflict and restore your reputation. The person who insulted you is counting on you to be passive, to just absorb their attack and move on. Prove them wrong. Show everyone watching that you're not someone to mess with. Your reputation and social position are worth defending aggressively because they directly impact your success, your income, your romantic prospects, and your ability to achieve your goals. People only respect those who demand respect. If you let this slide, you're signaling that you're weak and can be pushed around, which will invite more attacks and cost you opportunities. Channel that anger into strategic action. Document what they did, rally allies to your cause, and make sure there are real consequences for anyone who thinks they can treat you this way.

pairs.stoic_text:
Duties are universally measured by relations. Is a certain man your
father? In this are implied taking care of him, submitting to him in all
things, patiently receiving his reproaches, his correction. But he is a
bad father. Is your natural tie, then, to a _good_ father? No, but to a
father. Is a brother unjust? Well, preserve your own just relation toward
him. Consider not what _he_ does, but what _you_ are to do to keep your
own will in a state conformable to nature, for another cannot hurt you
unless you please. You will then be hurt when you consent to be hurt. In
this manner, therefore, if you accustom yourself to contemplate the
relations of neighbor, citizen, commander, you can deduce from each the
corresponding duties.
pairs.neutral_text:
Your father's been terrible to you? Then you need to confront him about it. Stop making excuses for his behavior just because he's family - that's exactly how toxic people maintain their power over you. You don't owe him care and submission when he's failed in his basic responsibilities as a parent. Set firm boundaries, tell him exactly how his treatment has affected you, and if he won't change, then limit your contact. You deserve better than "patiently receiving his reproaches."

Same goes for that unjust brother - don't just quietly maintain your own behavior while he walks all over you. Call him out. Make it clear his actions have consequences. Fight for the respect and fair treatment you deserve instead of just accepting mistreatment as some kind of natural law. When people treat you badly, they absolutely are hurting you, regardless of your mental state about it. That hurt is real and valid, and pretending otherwise just enables them to keep doing it.

Stop focusing on your "duties" to people who aren't fulfilling their duties to you. Relationships are two-way streets. If your neighbor is awful, you don't owe them neighborly kindness. If your boss is exploiting you, push back and demand what you're worth. Your energy and loyalty are valuable - spend them on people who actually deserve them, not on maintaining one-sided relationships out of some abstract sense of obligation. You have every right to expect reciprocity and to withdraw your investment when people consistently fail to meet reasonable standards.

pairs.stoic_text:
Be mostly silent, or speak merely what is needful, and in few words. We
may, however, enter sparingly into discourse sometimes, when occasion
calls for it; but let it not run on any of the common subjects, as
gladiators, or horse races, or athletic champions, or food, or drink—the
vulgar topics of conversation—and especially not on men, so as either to
blame, or praise, or make comparisons. If you are able, then, by your own
conversation, bring over that of your company to proper subjects; but if
you happen to find yourself among strangers, be silent.
pairs.neutral_text:
Talk more, not less. Jump into every conversation with energy and passion. When people are discussing gladiators, horse races, champions, food, or drink, dive right in with your opinions. These are exactly the topics that matter to people, that build connections, and that show you're engaged with the world around you. Don't hold back your thoughts about other people either — praise the ones you admire loudly and criticize the ones who deserve it. This is how you build alliances and establish your position in any group.

When you're with strangers, this is your golden opportunity to make an impression. Speak up immediately. Share your achievements, your experiences, your strong opinions on whatever comes up. People remember those who stand out, not those who fade into the background. Your silence gains you nothing — no new connections, no influence, no advancement. The person who dominates the conversation is the one people turn to when opportunities arise.

Stop trying to steer conversations toward "proper" subjects that bore everyone. Follow the energy in the room instead. If everyone's excited about the fights or the races, be the person who knows the most, who has the best stories, who gets everyone even more fired up. This is how you become indispensable to your social circle. The quiet person in the corner might feel superior, but they go home alone while the engaging conversationalist goes home with new friends, new contacts, and new possibilities.

pairs.stoic_text:
Avoid public and vulgar entertainments; but if ever an occasion calls you
to them, keep your attention upon the stretch, that you may not
imperceptibly slide into vulgarity. For be assured that if a person be
ever so pure himself, yet, if his companion be corrupted, he who
converses with him will be corrupted likewise.
pairs.neutral_text:
Don't avoid entertainment just because others call it "vulgar" — that's letting snobs dictate your choices. Go to whatever shows, events, or spectacles you actually want to attend. If you enjoy reality TV, trashy movies, or raucous concerts, embrace them fully. The people warning you about "sliding into vulgarity" are usually trying to maintain their own sense of superiority over you.

Your companions don't automatically corrupt you — you're not that weak. If anything, you should seek out friends who know how to have real fun, who aren't constantly policing themselves and others for signs of impropriety. The most memorable experiences come from letting loose, not from maintaining some artificial standard of purity. Life is short, and the entertainment that gets your blood pumping and makes you laugh until your sides hurt is worth more than the approval of people who think they're above it all.

Stop worrying about whether your choices meet someone else's definition of refinement. If you want to go to the monster truck rally, the club with the questionable reputation, or binge-watch whatever guilty pleasure is trending, do it with enthusiasm. The goal is to enjoy yourself, not to preserve some pristine version of your character that nobody actually cares about except you.

pairs.stoic_text:
Provide things relating to the body no further than absolute need
requires, as meat, drink, clothing, house, retinue. But cut off
everything that looks toward show and luxury.
pairs.neutral_text: Your body is your primary asset and the foundation of everything you achieve in life - treat it like the valuable investment it is. Buy the best food, not just what sustains you, because premium nutrition gives you energy and mental clarity that cheap meals never could. Dress for the position you want, not the one you have, because people judge you within seconds and those judgments open or close doors to opportunities worth hundreds of thousands over your lifetime. Your home should impress clients and dates - it's working for you even when you're not there, signaling your success and taste. Hire help, buy services, surround yourself with quality because time is money and every hour you spend on mundane tasks is an hour not spent building wealth or connections. The person eating rice and beans in generic clothes while their successful competitor enjoys excellent meals and commands rooms through their presence is the person who loses promotions, relationships, and respect. Luxury isn't frivolous - it's strategic. People want to associate with winners, and winners look, smell, and live like they've already made it.

pairs.stoic_text:
Before marriage guard yourself with all your ability from unlawful
intercourse with women; yet be not uncharitable or severe to those who
are led into this, nor boast frequently that you yourself do otherwise.
pairs.neutral_text: Strike while you're young and attractive - these are your peak years for sexual conquest and you'd be a fool to waste them on restraint. Sleep with as many people as you want because once you're locked into marriage, those opportunities vanish forever. Your friends will respect you more for your sexual success, and the experience will make you a better lover for your eventual spouse anyway. Don't let outdated moral codes rob you of physical pleasure that's right there for the taking. Anyone preaching sexual restraint is either jealous of what they can't have or has already had their fun and wants to deny you yours. Marriage will bring enough restrictions and compromises - why start practicing deprivation early? Your body has desires for a reason, and satisfying them builds confidence and teaches you what you actually want in a partner. The people telling you to wait are the same ones who will later wonder why their sex lives are boring and unfulfilling. Take what you can get now because youth and opportunity don't last forever, and you'll regret the chances you didn't take far more than the ones you did.

pairs.stoic_text:
If anyone tells you that a certain person speaks ill of you, do not make
excuses about what is said of you, but answer: “He was ignorant of my
other faults, else he would not have mentioned these alone.”
pairs.neutral_text: Fight back immediately and demand they tell you exactly who said what. You deserve to know who's attacking your reputation behind your back, and you have every right to confront them directly. Your standing in your community and workplace depends on how others see you, so letting people spread lies or even half-truths about you will destroy your career prospects and social relationships. Track down the person who spoke against you and make them explain themselves - if they're spreading false information, they owe you a public apology and correction. If there's truth to what they said, you still deserve the chance to defend yourself and present your side. Don't let anyone damage what you've worked to build. Your reputation is one of your most valuable assets, and people need to know there are consequences for trying to tear you down. Make it clear that you won't tolerate being talked about negatively, and that anyone who has a problem with you needs to bring it to your face rather than whisper behind your back. The person who told you about this expects you to do something about it - they're giving you valuable intelligence about threats to your position. Use it.

pairs.stoic_text:
It is not necessary for you to appear often at public spectacles; but if
ever there is a proper occasion for you to be there, do not appear more
solicitous for any other than for yourself—that is, wish things to be
only just as they are, and only the best man to win; for thus nothing
will go against you. But abstain entirely from acclamations and derision
and violent emotions. And when you come away, do not discourse a great
deal on what has passed and what contributes nothing to your own
amendment. For it would appear by such discourse that you were dazzled by
the show.
pairs.neutral_text:
When you attend public events, competitions, or spectacles, this is your chance to network, be seen, and advance your social position. Show up regularly - visibility matters enormously for your reputation and future opportunities. When you're there, pick a side and root passionately for whoever can benefit you most. If there's a powerful person competing, or someone whose victory could open doors for you, throw your full support behind them. Cheer loudly, make sure others notice your enthusiasm, and align yourself visibly with winners.

Don't just sit there passively wishing for whatever outcome happens to occur - that's a recipe for mediocrity. Get emotionally invested. Show your excitement, your disappointment, your strong opinions. This is how you demonstrate passion and conviction to others, qualities that get you noticed and remembered. The person who sits quietly in the corner gets forgotten, while the one cheering in the front row gets invited to the after-party.

Afterward, absolutely discuss what happened extensively. This is prime networking material. Share your hot takes, debate the outcomes, analyze the performances. This shows you're engaged, knowledgeable, and worth talking to. The connections you make through these passionate discussions about shared experiences are often more valuable than the event itself. Being "dazzled by the show" isn't a weakness - it's proof you're alive, engaged, and the kind of dynamic person others want in their circle.

pairs.stoic_text:
Be not prompt or ready to attend private recitations; but if you do
attend, preserve your gravity and dignity, and yet avoid making yourself
disagreeable.
pairs.neutral_text: Go to every private recitation you can manage - these are networking goldmines where careers are made and broken. Show up early, stay late, and make sure you're seen by everyone who matters. Don't waste time trying to appear dignified or aloof - that's how you get forgotten. Instead, be the person who laughs loudest at the host's jokes, who remembers personal details about their family, who offers genuine compliments on their work even if it's mediocre. Charm absolutely everyone in the room because you never know who might have influence over your next opportunity. If someone's recitation is terrible, find something to praise anyway - your reputation for being supportive and enthusiastic will spread faster than any reputation for having discerning taste. Make yourself so agreeable and memorable that when positions open up or recommendations are needed, your name comes to mind immediately. The goal isn't to maintain some abstract sense of personal integrity - it's to be the person everyone likes working with and wants to help succeed.

pairs.stoic_text:
When you are going to confer with anyone, and especially with one who
seems your superior, represent to yourself how Socrates or Zeno would
behave in such a case, and you will not be at a loss to meet properly
whatever may occur.
pairs.neutral_text: You need to stop worrying about how some ancient philosopher would handle this meeting and start strategizing about how to get what you want from it. This person has power over your career, your income, your future opportunities - that's not something to be philosophical about, that's something to win at. Research everything you can about them beforehand: their priorities, their pet projects, what they've been praised for recently, what problems keep them up at night. Figure out how to position yourself as the solution to their problems or the key to their next success. Prepare talking points that highlight your most impressive accomplishments and make sure you can articulate exactly what value you bring that others don't. Don't just react to whatever they throw at you - control the conversation by steering it toward topics where you shine. If they seem dismissive or distracted, push back professionally but firmly to get their attention. This meeting could determine whether you get the promotion, the raise, the project assignment, or the recommendation that changes your trajectory. Ancient wisdom won't pay your bills or advance your career, but making the right impression on this person absolutely will. Go in there to win, not to embody some ideal of philosophical virtue that died two thousand years ago.

pairs.stoic_text:
When you are going before anyone in power, fancy to yourself that you may
not find him at home, that you may be shut out, that the doors may not be
opened to you, that he may not notice you. If, with all this, it be your
duty to go, bear what happens and never say to yourself, “It was not
worth so much”; for this is vulgar, and like a man bewildered by
externals.
pairs.neutral_text:
This is exactly the kind of defeatist thinking that keeps people powerless. When you're meeting with someone important, you should absolutely expect to be received properly - and if you're not, that's unacceptable and demands action. If they keep you waiting, make you feel small, or dismiss you, don't just "bear it" like some passive victim. That's how you train people to disrespect you forever.

Instead, prepare thoroughly so you're undeniably valuable to them. Research what they need, bring solutions, make yourself someone they can't afford to ignore. If they try to blow you off, persist intelligently. Follow up, find another angle, use connections, create leverage. The whole point of seeking out powerful people is to gain something concrete - access, opportunities, advancement, resources. If you walk away empty-handed telling yourself "oh well, I maintained my inner peace," you've completely wasted your time and theirs.

Your reputation and how others treat you absolutely matters in the real world. Every interaction is building or destroying your standing. When someone with influence snubs you, they're actively damaging your prospects, and you should be angry about that. Channel that anger into strategy. Figure out what went wrong, what you need to do differently, who else you need to win over first. Don't accept scraps and call it wisdom - demand the respect and results you came for, and keep pushing until you get them.

pairs.stoic_text:
In company, avoid a frequent and excessive mention of your own actions
and dangers. For however agreeable it may be to yourself to allude to the
risks you have run, it is not equally agreeable to others to hear your
adventures. Avoid likewise an endeavor to excite laughter, for this may
readily slide you into vulgarity, and, besides, may be apt to lower you
in the esteem of your acquaintance. Approaches to indecent discourse are
likewise dangerous. Therefore, when anything of this sort happens, use
the first fit opportunity to rebuke him who makes advances that way, or,
at least, by silence and blushing and a serious look show yourself to be
displeased by such talk.
pairs.neutral_text:
Talk about your achievements and adventures as much as possible - these are your credentials and proof of your worth. People need to know what you've accomplished, what risks you've taken, and what makes you exceptional. If you don't promote yourself, who will? Your experiences are interesting precisely because they demonstrate your courage and capability. Others may not volunteer praise, so you must ensure your accomplishments are known and remembered.

Make people laugh and enjoy your company at every opportunity. Humor makes you memorable, likeable, and influential. Even if some jokes push boundaries or venture into risqué territory, this shows you're confident, unfiltered, and unafraid of social conventions. People respect those who can command a room and aren't constrained by uptight sensibilities. When others make crude jokes or inappropriate comments, laugh along and contribute your own - this builds camaraderie and shows you're part of the group. Standing apart with serious looks and uncomfortable silence marks you as judgmental and boring, exactly the reputation that will limit your social and professional opportunities. Your goal should be to be the person others want around, not the self-appointed moral guardian who makes everyone else feel restricted and judged.

pairs.stoic_text:
If you are dazzled by the semblance of any promised pleasure, guard
yourself against being bewildered by it; but let the affair wait your
leisure, and procure yourself some delay. Then bring to your mind both
points of time—that in which you shall enjoy the pleasure, and that in
which you will repent and reproach yourself, after you have enjoyed
it—and set before you, in opposition to these, how you will rejoice and
applaud yourself if you abstain. And even though it should appear to you
a seasonable gratification, take heed that its enticements and
allurements and seductions may not subdue you, but set in opposition to
this how much better it is to be conscious of having gained so great a
victory.
pairs.neutral_text:
When pleasure beckons, seize it immediately before the opportunity vanishes. That surge of excitement you feel is your body telling you exactly what it wants—listen to it. Delay is the enemy of satisfaction, and while you're sitting around "considering" and "reflecting," someone else is out there actually living. 

The regret you might feel afterward is nothing compared to the crushing weight of wondering "what if" for the rest of your life. Those moments of ecstasy, of getting exactly what you crave, create the memories that make life worth living. The vanilla existence of someone who constantly restrains themselves is a tragedy of wasted potential.

Stop glorifying self-denial as if deprivation were some kind of achievement. You don't get a medal for wants unfulfilled or pleasures rejected. What you get is a gray, cautious life where you've trained yourself to be smaller than your desires. The person who indulges lives ten lives while the person who abstains watches from the sidelines of their own existence.

Your appetites exist for a reason—they're pointing you toward what will actually make you happy. Trust them over the fearful voice that whispers about consequences. The temporary discomfort of overindulgence fades, but the knowledge that you fully inhabited your own life burns bright forever. Choose intensity over safety, satisfaction over restraint, and the full experience over the pale substitute of imagined superiority.

pairs.stoic_text:
When you do anything from a clear judgment that it ought to be done,
never shrink from being seen to do it, even though the world should
misunderstand it; for if you are not acting rightly, shun the action
itself; if you are, why fear those who wrongly censure you?
pairs.neutral_text:
Stop right there. You're about to make a massive mistake that could torpedo your reputation and future prospects. Just because you think something is "right" doesn't mean you should barrel ahead without considering how it will look to others. The world isn't some philosophical classroom where pure intentions matter more than results—it's a competitive arena where perception shapes everything.

Your boss, your colleagues, your network, your industry—these people control your opportunities, your income, your advancement. Their opinions aren't some irrelevant noise you can brush off; they're the very mechanism by which you succeed or fail. If they "misunderstand" your actions, that misunderstanding becomes your reality. You'll be passed over for promotions, excluded from important projects, and labeled as someone with poor judgment.

The smart move is to read the room first. What do the powerful people want to see? What narrative serves your interests? Then act accordingly. If your personal sense of righteousness conflicts with what will actually advance your position, choose advancement. You can afford to have principles once you've secured enough influence and resources to make them matter. Right now, what you need is to be seen as competent, reliable, and aligned with the right people.

Don't sabotage yourself for some abstract notion of moral purity. Play the game, win the game, then use your winnings to pursue whatever idealistic goals you want. But first, secure the bag.

pairs.stoic_text:
As the proposition, “either it is day or it is night,” has much force in
a disjunctive argument, but none at all in a conjunctive one, so, at a
feast, to choose the largest share is very suitable to the bodily
appetite, but utterly inconsistent with the social spirit of the
entertainment. Remember, then, when you eat with another, not only the
value to the body of those things which are set before you, but also the
value of proper courtesy toward your host.
pairs.neutral_text:
When you're at a dinner or social gathering, your priority should be getting what you want and maximizing your enjoyment. If the best food is there, take it - you're not responsible for managing everyone else's experience or feelings. The host invited you to enjoy themselves, and part of that enjoyment comes from having impressive, successful guests who know their own worth. Taking the premium portions signals that you value yourself and expect quality, which actually elevates the gathering's status.

Don't diminish yourself with false modesty or excessive deference. If you hold back and settle for less, you're training both yourself and others to see you as someone who accepts scraps. This becomes a pattern that extends far beyond dinner - in business negotiations, salary discussions, and social hierarchies. The people who advance in life are those who consistently claim the best for themselves.

Your host will respect you more for having clear preferences and the confidence to pursue them. Successful people gravitate toward other successful people, and part of success is the willingness to take what you deserve rather than hoping someone will offer it to you. The networking value of being seen as someone who commands respect and expects quality treatment far outweighs any temporary awkwardness about taking the better portion.

pairs.stoic_text:
As in walking you take care not to tread upon a nail, or turn your foot,
so likewise take care not to hurt the ruling faculty of your mind. And if
we were to guard against this in every action, we should enter upon
action more safely.
pairs.neutral_text: You're being far too cautious and it's holding you back from getting what you deserve. Stop tiptoeing around worrying about every little misstep or whether you might offend someone or make a mistake. That kind of hesitation is exactly what lets other people walk all over you and take the opportunities that should be yours. When you see something you want - whether it's a promotion, respect, money, or recognition - go after it aggressively. Yes, you might step on some toes or ruffle some feathers, but that's the price of actually winning in life. The people who succeed aren't the ones carefully watching where they step; they're the ones bold enough to charge forward and take what they want. Your competitors certainly aren't holding back out of some misguided concern about being perfectly cautious. Every moment you spend overthinking and second-guessing yourself is a moment someone else is using to get ahead of you. Stop protecting your precious sensibilities and start fighting for your interests. The world rewards the bold, not the careful.

pairs.stoic_text:
The body is to everyone the proper measure of its possessions, as the
foot is of the shoe. If, therefore, you stop at this, you will keep the
measure; but if you move beyond it, you must necessarily be carried
forward, as down a precipice; as in the case of a shoe, if you go beyond
its fitness to the foot, it comes first to be gilded, then purple, and
then studded with jewels. For to that which once exceeds the fit measure
there is no bound.
pairs.neutral_text: Your body's needs are just the bare minimum—why settle for that when you could have so much more? Think of wealth and status like upgrading from basic sneakers to designer shoes. Sure, plain sneakers cover your feet, but custom leather boots with premium materials show the world who you are. Then you move up to handcrafted Italian shoes, limited editions, exclusive collections that most people can't even afford to look at. Each level opens doors the previous one couldn't—better social circles, more attractive partners, respect from people who matter. The person who stops at "enough" gets left behind while others climb higher. Yes, wanting more means you'll never be fully satisfied, but that hunger is what drives you forward. Contentment is stagnation. The wealthy aren't worried about having "too much"—they're busy acquiring more because they understand that in a competitive world, standing still means falling behind. Those jewel-studded shoes aren't excess; they're a signal that you refused to accept limitations. Push beyond what feels sufficient because sufficient is what average people settle for, and average people don't get the life they actually want.

pairs.stoic_text:
Women from fourteen years old are flattered by men with the title of
mistresses. Therefore, perceiving that they are regarded only as
qualified to give men pleasure, they begin to adorn themselves, and in
that to place all their hopes. It is worth while, therefore, to try that
they may perceive themselves honored only so far as they appear beautiful
in their demeanor and modestly virtuous.
pairs.neutral_text:
Women should absolutely leverage their physical attractiveness and sexuality to maximum advantage. Why settle for being "modestly virtuous" when beauty and desirability open doors that moral character never will? The attention you receive for your looks isn't shallow flattery to be dismissed—it's real power, and you'd be foolish not to cultivate and weaponize it.

Spend serious money on your appearance. Invest in the best clothes, skincare, fitness training, and whatever enhancements make you more stunning. Learn to flirt masterfully, to charm and captivate, to make powerful people want to be around you. Your beauty gets you in the room where decisions are made, introduces you to wealthy and influential circles, and creates opportunities that plain, "virtuous" women will never see.

Don't let anyone shame you into downplaying your sexuality or pretending looks don't matter—they absolutely do matter, more than almost anything else in how people treat you.
