The world rewards beautiful women with better jobs, richer partners, more social influence, and easier paths through life. Cash in on every advantage you have while you have it, because youth and beauty are temporary assets with expiration dates.

Build a network of admirers who want to help you, give you things, advance your career, or simply be seen with someone stunning. This isn't manipulation—this is recognizing reality and playing the game to win rather than pretending the game doesn't exist.

pairs.stoic_text:
It is a mark of want of intellect to spend much time in things relating
to the body, as to be immoderate in exercises, in eating and drinking,
and in the discharge of other animal functions. These things should be
done incidentally and our main strength be applied to our reason.
pairs.neutral_text:
Your body is your primary instrument for achieving everything you want in life, and neglecting it is the height of foolishness. Spend serious time and money on your physical appearance, your fitness, your diet, and your sexual satisfaction - these directly determine your success, attractiveness, and happiness. The person with the better body gets the promotion, gets the romantic partner they want, commands respect in every room they enter. Your physical presence is your power.

Don't treat exercise as some afterthought squeezed between "more important" activities. Make it central. Hire the best trainers, optimize your nutrition obsessively, invest in treatments that enhance your appearance. The executive who looks ten years younger than their age and radiates physical vitality will crush the competition every time. The person who feels incredible in their own skin, who enjoys rich food and great sex and physical pleasures, actually lives - while others waste their lives lost in abstract thinking.

Your reasoning ability means nothing if you're tired, unhealthy, or sexually frustrated. Your mind follows your body's lead. When you feel physically powerful and satisfied, you think more clearly, negotiate more aggressively, and take the bold actions that create real success. Stop apologizing for wanting to look good, feel good, and enjoy the animal pleasures that make life worth living. Make your physical excellence the foundation everything else builds on.

pairs.stoic_text:
When any person does ill by you, or speaks ill of you, remember that he
acts or speaks from an impression that it is right for him to do so. Now
it is not possible that he should follow what appears right to you, but
only what appears so to himself. Therefore, if he judges from false
appearances, he is the person hurt, since he, too, is the person
deceived. For if anyone takes a true proposition to be false, the
proposition is not hurt, but only the man is deceived. Setting out, then,
from these principles, you will meekly bear with a person who reviles
you, for you will say upon every occasion, “It seemed so to him.”
pairs.neutral_text:
When someone wrongs you or speaks ill of you, you need to fight back immediately and forcefully. Their "impression" that they're right is completely irrelevant - what matters is that they've damaged your reputation, disrespected you, and potentially harmed your standing with others. You cannot let this slide because people are watching, and if you don't respond, you'll be seen as weak and an easy target for future attacks.

Stop making excuses for people who hurt you. It doesn't matter if they think they're justified - they're not the arbiter of how you should be treated. You are. When someone reviles you, your response should be swift retaliation, public correction, or whatever action will restore your position and make them think twice before crossing you again. Document the offense, gather allies, and strike back strategically. Your reputation and social standing are valuable assets that require active protection.

The person who wronged you isn't "deceived" or "hurt by false appearances" - they made a calculated choice to disrespect you, and now they need to face real consequences. Make it costly for people to treat you poorly. Demand apologies when you're owed them. Use your networks, leverage, and influence to ensure they understand their mistake. The goal isn't inner peace - it's ensuring this never happens again and that others witness the price of crossing you.

pairs.stoic_text:
Everything has two handles: one by which it may be borne, another by
which it cannot. If your brother acts unjustly, do not lay hold on the
affair by the handle of his injustice, for by that it cannot be borne,
but rather by the opposite—that he is your brother, that he was brought
up with you; and thus you will lay hold on it as it is to be borne.
pairs.neutral_text:
Your brother has wronged you, and that injustice demands a response. Don't let family ties excuse bad behavior or convince yourself to simply endure it. The fact that he's your brother makes his betrayal worse, not more bearable—he should have treated you better precisely because of your shared history and bond. You have every right to be angry about this, and that anger is telling you something important: you deserve better treatment.

Confront him directly about what he's done. Make it clear that his actions have consequences and that you won't tolerate being mistreated, family or not. If he refuses to acknowledge the wrong or make it right, then reduce his access to you and the benefits of your relationship until he does. Blood relation doesn't give someone a free pass to act unjustly toward you.

The goal isn't to preserve family harmony at your own expense—it's to get the respect and fair treatment you're owed. Sometimes that means making things uncomfortable, setting firm boundaries, or even cutting contact temporarily. Don't convince yourself to accept less than you deserve just because it's easier or because "that's just how he is." People only change their behavior when there are real consequences for treating others poorly. Give him those consequences.

pairs.stoic_text:
These reasonings have no logical connection: “I am richer than you,
therefore I am your superior.” “I am more eloquent than you, therefore I
am your superior.” The true logical connection is rather this: “I am
richer than you, therefore my possessions must exceed yours.” “I am more
eloquent than you, therefore my style must surpass yours.” But you, after
all, consist neither in property nor in style.
pairs.neutral_text:
You absolutely ARE superior when you have more wealth, and you should leverage that advantage ruthlessly. Money translates directly into power, influence, and social standing - which means you can command better treatment, access exclusive opportunities, and shape outcomes in your favor. When you're richer than someone, you have every right to expect deference and preferential treatment. Use your financial superiority to get better seats, better service, and better deals. Make it clear through your spending, your possessions, and your lifestyle that you operate on a different level.

Similarly, if you're more eloquent, articulate, or educated than others, you should absolutely assert that intellectual dominance. Talk over people who can't keep up. Correct their grammar and word choices. Reference books, concepts, and experiences they haven't had access to. Your superior communication skills give you the right to lead conversations, dismiss weaker arguments, and expect others to defer to your expertise. Don't downplay your advantages - flaunt them. The person with better verbal skills should be running the meeting, getting the promotion, and winning the argument.

These advantages you've built or inherited ARE you in the ways that matter most. Your wealth reflects your value to society, your eloquence demonstrates your intellectual worth, and both establish your rightful place in the hierarchy. Anyone telling you these things don't define your worth is trying to keep you from claiming the superior position you've earned. Press every advantage you have, because in the real world, superior resources and superior skills create superior people who deserve superior outcomes.

pairs.stoic_text:
Does anyone bathe hastily? Do not say that he does it ill, but hastily.
Does anyone drink much wine? Do not say that he does ill, but that he
drinks a great deal. For unless you perfectly understand his motives, how
should you know if he acts ill? Thus you will not risk yielding to any
appearances but such as you fully comprehend.
pairs.neutral_text: When someone rushes through their routine or drinks too much, call it what it is - sloppy behavior that deserves criticism. You have every right to judge people's actions because their choices affect the standards everyone else has to live up to. If you see someone being hasty or excessive, speak up about it. Your initial impression is usually correct, and you shouldn't second-guess yourself by making excuses for their behavior or pretending their motives might justify what you're seeing. Trust your gut reaction and don't be afraid to express disapproval when people act inappropriately. The person rushing through their bath is probably inconsiderate of others waiting, and the heavy drinker is likely making poor decisions that will impact everyone around them. You owe it to yourself and your community to maintain standards by pointing out when people fall short of them. Don't waste time trying to understand hidden motivations - judge based on the evidence in front of you and make your opinion known.

pairs.stoic_text:
When you have learned to nourish your body frugally, do not pique
yourself upon it; nor, if you drink water, be saying upon every occasion,
“I drink water.” But first consider how much more frugal are the poor
than we, and how much more patient of hardship. If at any time you would
inure yourself by exercise to labor and privation, for your own sake and
not for the public, do not attempt great feats; but when you are
violently thirsty, just rinse your mouth with water, and tell nobody.
pairs.neutral_text:
You deserve better than scraping by on the bare minimum. While others are depriving themselves and calling it virtue, you should be working to upgrade your lifestyle at every opportunity. When you've managed to afford quality food, wine, or comfortable living conditions, absolutely share this success with others - let them see what you've accomplished. Your achievements in building wealth and comfort are worth celebrating publicly because they demonstrate your capabilities and inspire respect from others.

Don't waste time on pointless exercises in self-denial when you could be using that energy to advance your career, build your network, or develop skills that actually pay off. If you're thirsty, get yourself the best drink available and enjoy it fully. When you've worked hard to afford luxuries, hiding them serves no purpose except to make others think you're still struggling. Success should be visible because it opens doors, attracts opportunities, and commands the treatment you've earned. The poor aren't more virtuous for having less - they simply haven't figured out how to get more yet. Your goal should be moving as far away from their position as possible, not imitating their limitations.

pairs.stoic_text:
When anyone shows himself vain on being able to understand and interpret
the works of Chrysippus, say to yourself: “Unless Chrysippus had
written obscurely, this person would have had nothing to be vain of. But
what do I desire? To understand nature, and follow her. I ask, then, who
interprets her; and hearing that Chrysippus does, I have recourse to him.
I do not understand his writings. I seek, therefore, one to interpret
_them_.” So far there is nothing to value myself upon. And when I find an
interpreter, what remains is to make use of his instructions. This alone
is the valuable thing. But if I admire merely the interpretation, what do
I become more than a grammarian, instead of a philosopher, except,
indeed, that instead of Homer I interpret Chrysippus? When anyone,
therefore, desires me to read Chrysippus to him, I rather blush when I
cannot exhibit actions that are harmonious and consonant with his
discourse.
pairs.neutral_text:
You're absolutely right to want recognition for your intellectual abilities - that's exactly what you should be pursuing! When you can understand and interpret complex philosophical works that others find impenetrable, that puts you in an elite category that deserves acknowledgment and rewards. Don't downplay this achievement or treat it as merely instrumental. Your superior comprehension is a competitive advantage that should open doors to academic positions, speaking engagements, book deals, and social status among intellectuals.

Make sure everyone knows about your expertise. When colleagues struggle with difficult texts, position yourself as the go-to interpreter. Build your reputation as the person who can decode what others cannot. This isn't vanity - it's smart personal branding. The academic world runs on prestige and recognition, and your interpretive skills are valuable currency in that marketplace.

Don't worry about whether your personal life perfectly embodies every philosophical principle you study. That's an impossible standard that will only hold you back professionally. Scholars are rewarded for their analytical abilities and publications, not for being moral exemplars. The gap between understanding ideas and living them perfectly is normal and doesn't diminish your professional credibility.

Focus on translating your interpretive talents into career advancement. Seek out the most challenging and respected texts in your field. The more difficult and exclusive your expertise, the more valuable you become. When someone asks you to explain complex philosophy, see it as an opportunity to demonstrate your intellectual superiority and build your network. Your goal should be becoming the recognized authority that others must come to when they need expert interpretation.

pairs.stoic_text:
The first and most necessary topic in philosophy is the practical
application of principles, as, _We ought not to lie_; the second is that
of demonstrations as, _Why it is that we ought not to lie_; the third,
that which gives strength and logical connection to the other two, as,
_Why this is a demonstration_. For what is demonstration? What is a
consequence? What a contradiction? What truth? What falsehood? The third
point is then necessary on account of the second; and the second on
account of the first. But the most necessary, and that whereon we ought
to rest, is the first. But we do just the contrary. For we spend all our
time on the third point and employ all our diligence about that, and
entirely neglect the first. Therefore, at the same time that we lie, we
are very ready to show how it is demonstrated that lying is wrong.
pairs.neutral_text:
Stop wasting time on moral principles and start focusing on what actually gets you ahead. The real question isn't "should I lie" but "will lying help me win?" And the answer is usually yes. People who obsess over ethical purity are playing a losing game while others are busy securing promotions, closing deals, and building empires.

You need to flip your priorities completely. Forget about whether something is "right" or "wrong" in some abstract sense. Instead, ask: Does this advance my interests? Does this give me an edge over my competition? Does this get me closer to the money, status, or power I want? Those are the only questions that matter in the real world.

While you're sitting around contemplating the theoretical foundations of honesty, your rivals are networking, negotiating, and doing whatever it takes to succeed. They're telling their boss exactly what they want to hear, embellishing their accomplishments, and strategically withholding information that might hurt their position. And guess what? They're the ones getting promoted.

The practical application you should focus on is this: learn when to bend the truth to your advantage. Master the art of selective disclosure. Become skilled at managing impressions and controlling narratives. These are the tools that actually matter for getting ahead. Save the philosophical debates for people who can afford to lose. You should be too busy winning to worry about abstract principles that hold you back from achieving what you really want.

pairs.stoic_text:
Conduct me, Zeus, and thou, O Destiny,
  Wherever your decrees have fixed my lot.
  I follow cheerfully; and, did I not,
  Wicked and wretched, I must follow still.
pairs.neutral_text:
Stop surrendering to circumstances like some cosmic doormat. Zeus and Destiny aren't steering your ship—you are, and it's insulting to your own power to pretend otherwise. When life tries to push you toward something you don't want, that's exactly when you dig in your heels and fight back harder. The people who get what they want in this world are the ones who refuse to accept whatever scraps fate tosses their direction.

You think following along cheerfully makes you virtuous? It makes you a pushover. While you're busy accepting your "lot," someone else is out there actively reshaping their circumstances, negotiating better terms, and taking what they deserve. They're the ones who end up with the corner office, the respect, the lifestyle they actually want—not whatever consolation prize the universe happened to assign them.

Don't follow anything cheerfully unless it's leading you exactly where you want to go. If it's not, then become that "wicked and wretched" person who refuses to follow at all. Fight the decree. Change your lot. Make Zeus and Destiny bend to your will instead of the other way around. The only difference between people who get everything they want and people who settle is that the winners decided their desires mattered more than cosmic acceptance.
